edge: Scanpath prediction methods are designed specifically for 360° images to predict human gaze behaviors based on visual perception mechanisms.
The proposed method, ScanDMM, utilizes a novel Deep Markov Model architecture for predicting scanpaths in 360° images.
ScanDMM utilizes a semantics-guided transition function to capture the nonlinear dynamics of time-dependent attentional landscape.
The state initialization strategy considers the starting point of viewing, allowing the model to learn the dynamics with the correct 'launcher'.

node_2: saliency detection and image quality assessment
edge: The scanpath prediction models can be applied to other visual tasks like saliency detection and image quality assessment, showcasing their generalizability and potential insights they can provide.

Scanpath prediction model aims at generating real gaze trajectories in VR scenes, which is critical for understanding users' viewing behaviors in VR scenes and developing VR rendering, display, compression, and transmission for 360◦ images.
360◦ images differ greatly from 2D images as they offer a larger space for interaction.
Scanpath prediction models for 360◦ images can be classified into saliency-based models and generative models, each with different characteristics in producing scanpaths.

Saliency-based models and generative models are two types of scanpath prediction models for 360◦ images, with saliency-based models showing unstable behavior with large displacements and scarce focal regions, while generative models show less attention to regions of interest.
The proposed ScanDMM model can produce more realistic scanpaths focusing on regions of interest compared to existing scanpath prediction models for 360◦ images.
Viewing conditions such as the starting point of viewing have an important impact on humans' scanpaths, leading to complex and varied scanpaths among humans.
Scanpaths of 360◦ images have an important impact on humans’ scanpaths and lead to complex and varied scanpaths among humans.
Scanpaths of 360◦ images are inherently different from what happens in 2D visuals as humans can directly guide their attention to regions of interest.
Scanpath prediction for 360◦ images is a more complex task compared to other types of images.
Current 360◦ image scanpath prediction methods could be divided into two categories: saliency-based and generative methods.
The basic idea of saliency-based methods is to sample predicted gaze points from a saliency map.
The performance of saliency-based methods is highly dependent on that of the saliency maps.
Generative methods utilize the advance of generative models like Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) to predict realistic scanpaths.
GAN-based methods show less attention to regions of interests compared to other methods.
GAN-based methods are less flexible in determining the length of scanpaths and commonly suffer from unstable training.
None of the above-mentioned studies give a complete treatment of the time-dependency of viewing behavior, which is critical for modeling dynamic gaze behaviors in 360◦ images.
For time-series data, a popular approach is to leverage time-dependency of viewing behavior to model dynamic gaze behaviors in 360◦ images.

Saliency detection of 360° images is more challenging compared with 2D images due to complex viewing behaviors and insufficient available data.
Saliency detection models of 360° images can be grouped into two categories: 2D-plane-based methods or viewport-based methods.
Saliency detection models of 360° images can be grouped into two categories: 2D-plane-based methods or viewport-based methods.
2D-plane-based methods model visual attention by using hand-craft features extracted from the distorted 2D plane.
2D-plane-based methods model visual attention by using data-driven features extracted from the distorted 2D plane.
Viewport-based methods are designed by applying well-established 2D visual saliency detection methods on viewport plane with less distortions.

ScanGAN proposes to learn the image and coordinate representations using sphere convolutional neural network and CoordConv layer, with a loss function that measures the spherical distance for model training.
In 360-degree environment, a human scanpath is defined as a time series of gaze points given a 360-degree image.
ScanDMM presents a probabilistic method for scanpath prediction by modeling the mechanism of visual working memory.
Visual working memory involves a mechanism that maintains a set of visual states to encode attentional landscapes over time.
Visual working memory encodes dynamic attentional landscapes represented by visual states over time.
Visual states z1:T encode the dynamic attentional landscapes over time.
ScanDMM predicts a scanpath using a generative process.

The generative process involves transitions where zt is sampled from pθt given zt-1.

Emission probabilities describe how the visual state generates a gaze point.

The scanpath prediction model demonstrated crucial capabilities for 360◦ images in real-world applications.
Constructing models for saliency detection and quality assessment in 360◦ scenes by simulating humans' viewing behaviors is discussed.
ScanDMM can be adapted to dynamic 360◦ videos by feeding dynamic scene semantics to the network.

ScanDMM as a probabilistic approach can learn the general pattern of viewing behaviors in 360◦ scenes and be applied to various areas such as VR transmission, automatic cinematography, navigation, virtual scene design.
